resource.resource_name: Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition
resource.resource_short_name: ACAG North American Regional Estimates with Composition V4.NA.03
resource.resource_description: The Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition (V4.NA.03) dataset presents estimated annual average fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution total and compositional mass concentrations. This dataset covers North America with 0.01 degree (~1 km) spatial resolution during 2000 to 2018. Concentrations are estimated using satellite-based air pollution observations combined with a chemical transport model and subsequent calibration to ground-based air pollution observations using geographically weighted regression. This dataset covers total PM2.5 and the following PM2.5 components: black carbon (BC), ammonium (NH4), nitrate (NO3), sulfate (SO4), organic matter, mineral dust, and sea salt. This dataset is intended for use in large-scale, long-term epidemiological studies and health impact assessments.

resource.resource_name: Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) Global/Regional Estimates
resource.resource_description: The Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) Global/Regional Estimates (V5.GL.04) dataset presents estimated monthly and annual average concentrations of fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution. The dataset provides global coverage during 1998 to 2022 with 0.01 degree (~1 km) and 0.1 degree (~10 km) spatial resolution. Summary data tables are also available at the national scale for all countries and at the sub-national scale (e.g., for states, provinces, or regions) for Canada, China, India, and the United States. Concentrations are estimated using satellite-based air pollution observations combined with a chemical transport model and subsequent calibration to ground-based air pollution observations using geographically weighted regression. This dataset can be used to study monthly or seasonal variations in PM2.5 concentrations and is intended for use in large-scale epidemiological studies and health impact assessments.

resource.resource_name: Center for Air, Climate, and Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model
resource.resource_description: The Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model (v1) dataset provides estimated annual average, population-weighted concentrations of criteria air pollutants for states, counties, Census tracts, and Census block groups in the contiguous United States during 1979 to 2020. This dataset is based on a land use regression model that incorporates data from ground-based regulatory monitors, satellite-based observations, and land use and land cover information. This dataset covers the following criteria air pollutants: particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). The CACES Empirical Model v1 dataset is intended to support various applications in public health, risk assessment, planning, and policy, and has been used in health effects and health disparities research.

resource_reference.resource_reference_text: Please cite as follows: This article includes concentration estimates developed by the Center for Air, Climate and Energy Solutions (CACES) using v1 empirical models as described in Kim et al., 2018 and Lu et al., 2024.

resource.resource_name: Airport Data and Information Portal
resource.resource_description: The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Airport Data and Information Portal (ADIP) is a comprehensive resource for accessing a wide range of airport-related data and information managed by the FAA. The portal provides essential information for various stakeholders, including airport operators, planners, engineers, and researchers, and supports various uses, such as designing new facility expansions, ensuring regulatory compliance, and assessing environmental impacts. Specifically, the ADIP provides location information (geographic coordinates) for all aircraft landing facilities (airports, heliports, balloonports, seaplane bases, gliderports, ultralight ports) in the United States and United States Territories during 1981 to 2023.

resource.resource_name: Global Roads Open Access Data Set
resource.resource_description: The Global Roads Open Access Data Set Version 1 (gROADSv1) provides road information with global coverage. This dataset harmonizes road data available from public sources for each country. The dataset is a composite of road information during 1980 to 2010, depending on the available data for each country. The gROADSv1 dataset provides road locations (lines) and their harmonized functional classifications (highway, primary road, secondary road, tertiary road, local road, trail, private road).

resource.resource_name: Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Basic Data Files
resource.resource_description: The Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Basic Data Files provide annual estimated toxic releases (i.e., pollutant mass emissions to air, water, and land) for specific pollutants that are required by regulations to be reported to the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) by federal and industrial facilities. This includes industries involved in manufacturing, metal mining, electric power generation, chemical manufacturing, or hazardous waste treatment. The inventory includes chemicals that may cause cancer, significant adverse acute or chronic human health effects, or significant adverse environmental effects. The TRI covers the United States and United States Territories during 1987 to 2023. The TRI provides the point locations (geographic coordinates) of polluting industrial facilities and their facility-reported annual estimated mass emissions by pollutant and release type (air, water, or land). The TRI includes more than 800 pollutants (e.g., benzene, ethylbenzene, xylene, toluene).

resource.resource_name: Di et al. (2019) Daily and Annual PM2.5 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, v1.10 (2000 – 2016)
resource.resource_description: The Daily and Annual PM2.5 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, Version 1.10 (2000-2016) dataset contains fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution concentration estimates. PM2.5 concentrations are estimated using an ensemble modeling approach with three machine learning models (neural network, random forest, and gradient boosting) and a general additive model. The approach incorporates satellite data, meteorological variables, land use variables, elevation, chemical transport models, and other data sources. This dataset provides daily and annual average PM2.5 concentration estimates during 2000 to 2016 for the contiguous United States with 1 km spatial resolution.

resource.resource_name: Di et al. (2020) Daily and Annual NO2 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, v1.10 (2000 – 2016)
resource.resource_description: The Daily and Annual NO2 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, Version 1.10 (2000-2016) dataset contains nitrogen dioxide (NO2) air pollution concentration estimates. NO2 concentrations are estimated using an ensemble modeling approach with three machine learning models (neural network, random forest, and gradient boosting) and a generalized additive model. The approach incorporates satellite-based observations of NO2 column concentrations, land use and meteorological variables, chemical transport models, and other data sources. This dataset provides daily and annual average NO2 concentration estimates during 2000 to 2016 for the contiguous United States with 1 km spatial resolution.

resource.resource_description: The Daily 8-Hour Maximum and Annual O3 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, Version 1.10 (2000-2016) dataset contains estimated ozone (O3) air pollution concentrations. O3 concentrations are estimated using a geographically weighted ensemble modeling approach with three types of machine learning models (neural network, random forest, and gradient boosting). The approach incorporates O3 air pollution observations from ground-based monitors, land use and meteorological variables, chemical transport models, and remote sensing data, along with other sources. This dataset provides daily 8-hour maximum average and annual 8-hour maximum average O3 concentration estimates during 2000 to 2016 for the contiguous United States with 1 km spatial resolution.

resource.resource_description: The Daily and Annual PM2.5, O3, and NO2 Concentrations at ZIP Codes for the Contiguous U.S., 2000-2016, v1.0 dataset contains predictions of air pollution concentrations at the ZIP Code-level for 2000-2016. An ensemble framework consisting of three machine-learning models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Neural Network) was implemented to estimate the daily concentrations of fine particulate matter (PM2.5), ozone (O3), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2) in grid cells at 1-km resolution. Prediction variables included air monitoring data, satellite aerosol optical depth, meteorological conditions, chemical transport model simulations, and land-use variables. Annual predictions represent the average daily predictions in each grid cell for each year.

resource.resource_description: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Fused Air Quality Surface Using Downscaling (EPA FAQSD) presents modeled PM2.5 (daily average) and O3 (daily 8-hr maximum) surfaces. Predictions are generated from a Bayesian space-time downscaling fusion model that uses both air quality monitoring data from the National Air Monitoring Stations/State and Local Air Monitoring Stations and numerical output data from the EPA Community Multiscale Air Quality (CMAQ) database. resource.resource_name: Brokamp et al. (2019) Community Deprivation Index
resource.resource_description: The Community Deprivation Index (CDI) dataset contains a deprivation index for each census tract in the United States based on a principal components analysis of six different 2015 American Community Survey measures: population below poverty level, median income, high school education, health insurance status, assistance income, and vacant housing. The first component, poverty level, explains over 60% of the total variance compared to the other five measures. It is referred to as the “Deprivation Index”. Rescaling and normalization were used to scale the index to a range from 0 to 1, with 1 representing the most deprivation.

publication.citation: Brokamp, C., Beck, A. F., Goyal, N. K., Ryan, P., Greenberg, J. M., & Hall, E. S. (2019). Material community deprivation and hospital utilization during the first year of life: an urban population–based cohort study. Annals of epidemiology, 30, 37-43.

resource.resource_name: Beck et al. (2023) Köppen-Geiger Global 1-km Climate Classification Maps
resource.resource_short_name: Beck et al. (2023) Köppen-Geiger Climate Classification V2
resource.resource_description: The Beck et al. (2023) Köppen-Geiger Global 1-km Climate Classification Maps (Version 2) cover global land surfaces with spatial resolution of 0.01 degrees (~1 km). Additional maps are available for coarser spatial resolutions of 0.1 degrees, 0.5 degrees, and 1 degree. Maps cover six approximately 30-year time periods, including four historic periods (1901-1930, 1931-1960, 1961-1990, and 1991-2020) and two future time periods (2041-2070 and 2071-2099). The historic climate classifications are derived from multiple observation-based datasets of air temperature and precipitation. The future projected climate classifications are based on downscaled and bias-corrected air temperature and precipitation projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) for seven different socioeconomic scenarios (Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSPs)). Köppen-Geiger climate classifications include five major classes (tropical, arid, temperate, cold, and polar) and 30 sub-classes. The Köppen-Geiger climate classifications can be used for assessing environmental change, ecological modeling, and other applications that require information on variation in climate classification at local to global scales.

resource_reference.resource_reference_text: Beck, H.E., T.R. McVicar, N. Vergopolan, A. Berg, N.J. Lutsko, A. Dufour, Z. Zeng, X. Jiang, A.I.J.M. van Dijk, D.G. Miralles. (2023). High-resolution (1 km) Köppen-Geiger maps for 1901–2099 based on constrained CMIP6 projections. Scientific Data, 10(1), 724.

publication.citation: Beck, H.E., T.R. McVicar, N. Vergopolan, A. Berg, N.J. Lutsko, A. Dufour, Z. Zeng, X. Jiang, A.I.J.M. van Dijk, D.G. Miralles. (2023). High-resolution (1 km) Köppen-Geiger maps for 1901–2099 based on constrained CMIP6 projections. Scientific Data, 10(1), 724.

data_resource.time_available_comment: Historic data is available during the time period from 1901 to 2020. Future projections are available during the time period from 2041 to 2099.
example_metrics: Köppen-Geiger climate classification for the grid cell containing the geocoded address.

project.project_name: Gridded Population of the World Version 4

resource.resource_name: Gridded Population of the World (GPWv4) Population Density Adjusted to Match 2015 Revision United Nations World Population Prospects Country Totals
resource.resource_description: The Gridded Population of the World (GPWv4) Population Density Adjusted to Match 2015 Revision United Nations World Population Prospects (UN WPP) Country Totals (Version 4.11) dataset presents global population density estimates at 5-year intervals during 2000 to 2020. The gridded population density estimates are available for multiple spatial resolutions for ease of integration with other gridded earth science and remote sensing datasets. The following spatial resolutions are available: 30 arcseconds (~1 km), 2.5 arcminutes, 15 arcminutes, 30 arcminutes, and 1 degree. The estimates are based on country-specific census and population registers and are adjusted to match the country population totals from the UN WPP. The GPWv4 population density dataset has been used in various applications combining population and environmental data, such as vulnerability mapping, disaster impact assessments, and research on environmental changes related to human health. The dataset is also intended for use in developing geospatial models.

resource.resource_description: The National Risk Index (NRI) for Natural Hazards dataset helps identify United States communities most at risk to 18 types of natural hazards including, riverine flood, coastal flood, hurricane, and wildfire. resource.resource_name: Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder)
resource.resource_description: The Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. The datasets are acquired through collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations from satellite, airborne, and ground-based instruments that monitor air quality. The resources available can aid with studies to forecast poor air quality, track trace gases, visualize air quality trends, and forecast the movement of pollutants. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

resource.resource_name: Air Quality System (AQS)
resource.resource_description: The EPA's Air Quality System (AQS) is a comprehensive network of monitoring stations that collects and distributes data on various air pollutants. The monitoring stations provide hourly measurements of pollutants such as nitrogen dioxide (NO2), ozone (O3), and particulate matter (PM) (Lamsal et al., 2008). The data are considered the gold standard for measuring air pollutants (Considine et al., 2023). The EPA's efforts to improve air quality are guided by the Clean Air Act, which requires the agency to evaluate the costs and benefits of new rules and regulations to reduce pollution (McGuffey, 2016).

resource.resource_name: AirNow
resource.resource_description: AirNow is a comprehensive repository of air quality data collected by the U.S. EPA from thousands of monitoring stations across the U.S. and worldwide. It provides real-time and historical data based on the daily Air Quality Index (AQI) readings for key air pollutants in the Clean Air Act and is used by researchers, policymakers, and public health officials to support their initiatives. The data are also available to the public to help raise awareness of air pollution and its related health impacts. By providing accurate and up-to-date data, AirNow empowers individuals to make informed decisions regarding  outdoor activities to protect their health. AirNow partners include other federal agencies (e.g., NOAA, NASSA, CDC), U.S. tribal, state, and local agencies, and Canadian and Mexican partners.

resource.resource_name: Biosphere Data Pathfinder (Biosphere-Path)
resource.resource_description: The Biosphere Data Pathfinder (Biosphere-Path) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It has four components 1) Vegetation Characteristics and Processes Data, 2) Biodiversity-related Spectroscopy Data, 3) Human Impacts Data, and 4) Species Distribution Modeling. Together, they provide access to vegetation health (such as canopy height and evapotranspiration), spectroscopy, and species distribution modeling data, as well as data for monitoring human-induced changes that allow users to monitor biodiversity and forecast changes to ecological communities.

resource.resource_name: Diseases/Epidemics Data Pathfinder (DE-Pathfinder)
resource.resource_description: The Diseases/Epidemics Data Pathfinder (DE-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It is the result of collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations to provide information to help understand how environmental changes can lead to disease emergence, transmission, and outbreaks. Remote sensing data are used to map, monitor, and predict areas or regions at risk for disease outbreaks. Satellite imagery, coupled with ground-based data, aids in understanding many natural phenomena and human behaviors. The data products, which are openly available, have been used to understand disease outbreaks such as malaria, Rift Valley fever, and COVID-19, and how changes in human behavior are having impacts on the environment. The World Health Organization notes that disease outbreaks are often associated with environmental conditions. Changes in water and air quality, temperature, and precipitation for example, can affect disease transmission.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS)
resource.resource_description: The Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS) distributes Near Real-Time active fire data from the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) aboard the Aqua and Terra satellites, and the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) aboard S-NPP and NOAA 20 (formally known as JPSS-1). Globally these data are available within 3 hours of satellite observation, but for the U.S. and Canada active fire detections are available in real-time.

resource.resource_description: FIREX FireLab 2016 (FIREX) is a product of the NOAA Chemical Sciences Laboratory (CSL) that focuses on measuring fuels or combustion conditions that are characteristic of the western U.S. that may be under-sampled by the fire research community. Data from measurements of gas and particle phase emissions and processing from FIREX are freely available to the public and scientific community to support technology transfer, inform decision makers, and contribute to assessments and reports. resource.resource_name: Landslides Data Pathfinder
resource.resource_description: The Landslides Data Pathfinde is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It is designed for researchers, decision-makers, and the public to help better understand the interaction of complex events that cause landslides. The data, available from the datasets and tools provided, include information about past landslides, erosion, and fluvial processes in sloped areas. It includes data from various agency members of the Committee on Earth Observation Satellites. The information is used to help monitor and mitigate landslide hazards.

resource.resource_name: GOESR Advanced Baseline Imaging Products (GRABIPRD)
resource.resource_description: The Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellites R-series (GOES-R) Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) offers superior imaging of the Earth’s environment, oceans, and weather compared to previous instruments. The ABI uses 16 different spectral channels to capture data and is the primary instrument used on the GOES-R series satellites for scanning the Western hemisphere. These channels provide four times greater spatial resolution, and more than five times the temporal coverage in near-real-time. GOES-16 scans the Midwest and east coast of the continental U.S. and GOES-18 scans the west coast and Alaska. The data captured by the ABIs are used in 30 NOAA products that are grouped into ten basic types: Clouds, Aerosols, Atmospheric Vertical Profiles, Precipitation, Winds, Land, Ocean, Radiation, and Lightning. All of the data are available from the Comprehensive Large Array Data Stewardship System (CLASS) which offers search and filter capabilities to select specific data of interest. Due to the particularly large size of the data files, data must be requested through CLASS.

resource.resource_name: Head Start Center Locator
resource.resource_description: The Head Start program offers two applications for locating grant recipient services across the United States. One is the Head Start Center Locator (HSCL) which allows users to search HSCL based on the center type by state, city, or zip code. The locations for all Centers found in the requested area are displayed on an interactive map. The search results, with detailed information about each Center, is displayed below the map. Access to annual reports is also available from the More about this grantee link provided for each Center. The types of Centers include 1) Head Start Preschool, 2) Early Head Start, 3) Migrant/Seasonal Head Start, and 4) Native American/Alaska Native Head Start. The second application for finding services is the Head Start Service Location (HSSL) datasets, which is also searchable by state, city, or zip code based on the four center types shown above. The search results are less detailed and are only available for download as CSV, XML, or JSON files. The files include grant recipient name, service location and address, and the type of program provided, among other data. The information maintained in these datasets is vital to tracking the Head Start outreach and determining where and how early childhood development can be improved.

resource.resource_name: Blended Global Biomass Burning Emissions Product - Extended V.3 (GBBEPxV3)
resource.resource_description: The Blended Global Biomass Burning Emissions Product – Extended, version 3 (GBBEPx V3) system uses advanced algorithms to combine satellite-based observation data from three biomass burning emissions datasets to produce blended global daily biomass burning emission estimates. The datasets include MODIS Quick Fire Emission Dataset (QFED), VIIRS (NPP and JPSS-1) fire emissions, and Global Biomass Burning Emission Product from Geostationary satellites. Emission estimates are calculated for atmospheric pollutants released during wildfires and agricultural burnings. These pollutants include PM2.5, ammonia (NH3), black carbon (BC), carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), organic carbon (OC), and sulfur dioxide (SO2). The data, displayed on a global map at 0.1 x 0.1 degree spatial resolution, cover the current 30-day period. Additional system output includes fire detection records, spatial pattern of PM2.5 emissions, and continental statistics for PM2.5.

resource.resource_name: CDC Data Catalog
resource.resource_description: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) Data Catalog is a centralized resource of health-related datasets and information that is publicly accessible. It includes a wide range of datasets collected by the CDC on public health topics such as disease prevalence, health behaviors, environmental factors, and demographic information. The catalog is searchable using free text and search filters for data type, e.g., Datasets, Maps, Charts, Forms, and/or category, e.g., Chronic Diseases (which includes numerous sub-categories), Environmental Health and Toxicology, Health Statistics, to name a few. Data from the datasets are downloadable for use in research, analysis, and decision-making to address public health issues. Metadata and documentation that describe the context, methodology, and limitations of the data are also available.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES)
resource.resource_description: The Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model (v1) dataset provides estimated annual average, population-weighted concentrations of criteria air pollutants for states, counties, Census tracts, and Census block groups in the contiguous United States during 1979 to 2020. This dataset is based on a land use regression model that incorporates data from ground-based regulatory monitors, satellite-based observations, and land use and land cover information. This dataset covers the following criteria air pollutants: particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). The CACES Empirical Model v1 dataset is intended to support various applications in public health, risk assessment, planning, and policy. It has been used in health effects and health disparities research projects. Novel measurement and modeling approaches can be applied to understand spatial and temporal differences in human exposures and health outcomes.

resource.resource_name: Climate Risk and Resilience Portal (CRRPort)
resource.resource_description: The Climate Risk and Resilience Portal (ClimRR) allows visualization of climate projections for the United States at a small geographic scale. The projections characterize three decadal time periods, historical (1995-2004), mid-century (2045-2054), and end-of-century (2085 – 2094)] for two carbon emission scenarios, referred to as Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5 (emissions peak around 2040, then decline) ) and RCP 8.5 (emissions rates continue to rise). Climate projection data from three Global Climate Models that offer spatial resolutions from 100 – 200 km are combined, then a regional weather model for North America, the Weather Research and Forecasting model, is used to downscale the combined data to 12 km grid cells across the United States. The data include extreme temperatures (hot and cold), cooling and heating degree days, heat index, wind, fire weather index, and precipitation/no precipitation, which can be seen in different map layers. Data may also be viewed based on different Resilience map layers, e.g., Census Tracts: Population Age 65 and Older. Reports based on the viewing options selected are generated as PDF or CSV files. In addition, a national map for each data category shown above are available to visualize hazard layers alongside key resilience indicators, regional boundaries, and more. The insight into climate risks that ClimRR offers city and community leaders can help them prioritize vulnerable areas that need the most attention and future funding to improve resilience.

resource.resource_name: Consolidated Human Activity Database (CHAD)
resource.resource_description: The Consolidated Human Activity Database (CHAD) was initiated to compile pre-existing data from numerous human activity studies at the city, state and national levels. It was created to support human exposure, intake dose, and risk modeling by providing accurate information on daily human activities. CHAD was made publicly available in 2017 and includes data from studies conducted by a variety of government, private, and academic groups that date back to 1982. Data have been updated routinely throughout its development, with the most recent update occurring in 2020. The activity-data in CHAD encompasses a wide range of demographics, e.g., age, sex, employment, education, as well as records of personal activities and human behaviors that are recorded daily by study participants. These behaviors and activities are further broken down into individual hours and activity types. This allows researchers to examine specific groups within the general population to investigate how their daily activities influence exposure to chemicals in the environment. CHAD is routinely used in human health studies by EPA and other government agencies, academia, and private organizations to model human exposures and related risks.

resource.resource_name: EM-DAT The International Disaster Database
resource.resource_description: The EM-DAT International Disaster Database contains records of worldwide natural disasters and their health and economic impacts at a country level. Disasters are commonly considered sudden, unforeseen events which cause significant damage, destruction, and human suffering that require more than local resources to manage. They necessitate external assistance at the national or international level and may include droughts, earthquakes, floods, landslides, storms, and wildfires among others. The core data in EM-DAT are obtained from numerous sources such as UN agencies, non-governmental organizations, insurance companies, research institutes, and media outlets. The University of Louvain, Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters compiles the data, maintains the database, and freely distributes the data for non-commercial use. The database contains data from 1900 to the present that are available through three data products: 1) the primary resource - living EM-DAT Public Data, updated weekly; 2) the EM-DAT archive, pre-2000 data; and 3) The EM-DAT, Humanitarian Data Exchange, Country Profiles - yearly summaries of disaster data by country.

resource.resource_name: Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) Model
resource.resource_description: The Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) model is a tool developed by EPA designed to help assess and evaluate the potential risks posed by environmental pollution. It incorporates publicly available EPA Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) data to estimate the potential health and environmental risks from hazardous chemicals that are released into the environment. The model provides aggregated data from more than 30 years of TRI reporting. Disaggregated results are also available in the RSEI Geographic Microdata. The RSEI model is particularly valuable for understanding the broad-scale impact of industrial pollution on environmental and public health and helping target efforts for pollution reduction and risk mitigation.

resource.resource_name: Clean Air Status and Trends Network (CASNET)
resource.resource_description: The Clean Air Status and Trends Network (CASTNET) is an atmospheric monitoring network that was established in response to the 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments. Air sampling sites are located throughout the U.S. and Canada to measure trends in pollutant concentrations, atmospheric deposition, and ecological effects due to changes in air pollutant emissions. The sites measure weekly ambient concentrations of pollutants that are used to monitor and assess emission reduction programs. Data from CASTNET also support assessment of the primary and secondary National Ambient Air Quality Standards for ozone, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and PM2.5. Raw data as well as prepackaged data sets are publicly available for download. Over the past 30 years, CASNET data have helped inform environmental policy decisions resulting in a substantive improvement in air quality.

resource.resource_name: EPA Ecoregions
resource.resource_description: The EPA Ecoregions (ECOR) is a classification system of ecological regions based on similarities and differences in the mosaic of biotic, abiotic, terrestrial, and aquatic components, e.g., climate, vegetation, soil type, landforms, that can affect the quality and integrity of an ecosystem. They are organized into four levels, according to the extent of detail used to define each level. The levels range from Level I, which includes 15 general regions, to Level IV, the most detailed level with >900 finely detailed regions. Maps of North American showing ecoregions Levels I, II, and III are presented with Levels II and III regions nested and displayed inside Level I. Level II has 50 regions that provide an overview of ecological patterns at a national and subcontinental level and Level III has 182 ecoregions which allows more specific ecological characteristics to be defined and enhances environmental monitoring and reporting. Maps of the continental U.S. are also presented which display ecoregions Level III and IV as well as options to view the same two ecoregions in each of the 10 EPA regions or for individual states. Level IV, that provides the finest level of detail, is useful in environmental analysis and monitoring at the local level. All of the maps offered, and their corresponding GIS files, are available for download.

resource.resource_name: National Emissions Inventory (NEI)
resource.resource_description: The National Emissions Inventory (NEI) reports detailed, comprehensive estimates of air emissions for criteria pollutants, criteria precursors, and hazardous air pollutants every three years (e.g., 2011, 2014, 2017, and 2020). The majority of the data are submitted by State, Local, and Tribal air agencies using the EPA Emissions Inventory System. These data may also be supplemented by EPA-estimates using data from sources such as the Toxic Release Inventory and other programs. The key elements reported in NEI include sources, pollutants, geographic, temporal, and emissions components that represent emissions estimates from four primary source categories: Point (large sources located at a fixed, stationary location, e.g., industrial facilities and electric power plants); Nonpoint (sources too small to report as point sources, e.g., residential heating, commercial combustion); Onroad (on road gasoline, diesel, and other fueled vehicles); and Nonroad (off-road mobile sources that use gasoline, diesel, and other fuels, e.g., equipment for construction, lawn and garden, and aircraft ground support). Starting in 2020, wild land fire emission estimates are being aggregated to annual and county levels and included as nonpoint source data. An NEI Exploration tool is available to help users examine and retrieve data of interest.

project.project_name: Earth Science Data and Information System (ESDIS)

resource.resource_name: Gateway to NASA Earth Observation Data
resource.resource_description: The Earth Science Data and Information Systems (ESDIS) Program provides researchers, decision makers, and the public open access to NASA’s archive of Earth science data. The data and related tools help increase the understanding of environmental changes. Data are accessed from the Gateway to NASA Earth Observation Data (GNEOD) using one of the four search or browse options: the Data Catalog, Data Tools, browse by Platform, or browse by Pathfinder Topic. The Data Catalog can be searched by keywords, data collection platform, dataset name, etc. Search results, or the entire dataset collection, can also be filtered by specific parameters such as topic, observation method, and temporal or spatial parameters, to name a few. Pathfinder Topics guide users to ESDS topic-specific datasets (6 of which are included in the CHORDS Catalog). The 10 topics include: Atmosphere, Land Surface, Biosphere, Ocean, Climate Indicators, Solid Earth, Cryosphere Sun-Earth Interactions, Human Dimensions, and Terrestrial Hydrosphere. Data Tools provide access to a variety of tools for data discovery and visualization. Three tools are featured: 1) Worldview – allows browsing global imagery and downloading underlying data; 2) Vertex - a graphical search interface of synthetic aperture radar land use/cover data; and 3) Giovanni - Geospatial Interactive Online Visualization ANd aNalysis Infrastructure.

project.project_name: NOAA GOES-R Series Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) Level 2 Fire/Hot Spot Characterization (FDC)

resource.resource_name: NOAA GOES-R Series Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) Level 2 Fire/Hot Spot Characterization (FDC)
resource.resource_description: The GOES-R Series Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) Level 2, Fire/Hot Spot Characterization product (GOES-R_FDC) uses data collected by the NOAA, GOES-16 and GOES-17 satellites for the Western Hemisphere, which are processed by the GOES-R Algorithm Working Group in preparation for use by GOES-R_FDC. Data are displayed using four images. One is a fire mask that shows fire location, and the other three are pixel values that estimate fire size, temperature, and radiative power. The output is a kind of pixel level metadata that is useful in real-time and offline model integration and assessment. The pixel values in the fire mask image are also used to flag fire category and diagnostic information associated. The categories include Good quality fire pixel; Saturated fire pixel; Cloud contaminated fire pixel; High probability fire pixel; Medium probability fire pixel; Low probability fire pixel. The words “temporally filtered” precede the category name when the pixels are in close proximity for both space and time. The product image is displayed at 2 km resolution.

resource.resource_name: Meteorological Assimilation Data Ingest System (MADIS)
resource.resource_description: NOAA’s Meteorological Assimilation Data Ingest System (MADIS) is a comprehensive, centralized data integration system developed to collect, quality-control, and distribute real-time observational data from a wide range of in weather forecasting, climate research, and emergency response sources. Data from both NOAA and non-NOAA sources, such as surface weather stations, aircraft, radar, satellite, and ocean buoys, are decoded and then all of the observational data are encoded into a common format with uniform observation units and time stamps. Rigorous automated quality checks on all incoming data identify and flag anomalies or errors to allow users to inspect these data and decide whether to exclude them from analyses. As of March 2023, MADIS became fully operational under the management of the National Weather Service (NWS). Access to real-time and archived observational data plus real-time surface analyses grids is available via the online Data Application MADIS has played a key role in improving short-term weather forecasts, enhancing situational awareness during severe weather events, supporting climate monitoring, and aiding in transportation and public safety operations across the United States.

resource.resource_name: The Tropospheric Emissions: Monitoring of Pollution (TEMPO)
resource.resource_description: The Tropospheric Emissions: Monitoring of Pollution (TEMPO) instrument was designed for NASA 's mission to monitor air pollution over North America with high temporal and spatial resolution. It represents a significant advancement in how scientists observe and understand air quality. TEMPO was launched aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket on April 7, 2023, hosted on the Intelsat 40e satellite. TEMPO is mounted on a commercial communications satellite in geostationary orbit (~35,786 km above the equator), allowing it to continuously observe the same area of the Earth, specifically North America, and collect data in near-real-time.

resource.resource_description: ArboNET is a national surveillance system managed by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that tracks arboviral diseases (arthropod-borne viruses) in the United States. It monitors diseases transmitted by mosquitos, ticks, and other vectors. ArboNET collects data on human cases, non-human cases (birds, horses, and mosquitoes), and environmental factors. resource.resource_name: National Digital Forecast Database (NDFD)
resource.resource_description: The National Digital Forecast Database (NDFD) is a suite of gridded digital forecasts of basic weather elements (e.g., cloud cover, maximum temperature) that is generated by the National Weather Service (NWS) in collaboration with regional NWS Weather Forecast Offices (WFOs) and the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP). Data obtained from WFOs and NCEP are combined to create the NDFD digital forecasts. The gridded data are presented as map displays for weekly forecast periods with links at each location to graphical representations of the data along with a forecast discussion. Access to download data as General Regularly-distributed Information in Binary (GRIB) formatted files is also available using ftp and https transfer protocols or the AWS S3 Explorer (https://noaa-ndfd-pds.s3.amazonaws.com/index.html).

resource.resource_name: National Hydrography Dataset Plus Version 2.1 (NHDplusV2)
resource.resource_description: The National Hydrography Dataset Plus Version 2.1 (NHDplusV2) provides a national geospatial surface-water framework that is used by geospatial analysts and modelers to support their water resource applications. It is an enhanced version of NHDplusV1 produced by the U.S. EPA Office of Water in partnership with the U.S. Geological Survey. NHDplusV2 is an integrated suite of application-ready geospatial data products that integrates detailed hydrologic features such as streams, rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and watersheds with attributes related to flow direction, flow accumulation, and watershed boundaries. It utilizes and extends the best features of the National Hydrography Dataset (NHD), the National Elevation Dataset (NED), and the National Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD). The spatial resolution for NHD and NED have been improved to 1:100k and NED 30m, respectively. NHDplusV2 integrates additional attributes such as elevation, land use, and climate to enhance hydrological modeling, watershed analysis, flood forecasting, and water resource management. Improvements to catchment characteristics, headwater node areas, and cumulative drainage characteristics have been implemented along with advanced methods for estimating monthly and annual river and stream flow volumes and velocities. NHDplus2 is a vital resource for government agencies, decision makers, and researchers.

resource.resource_name: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Atmosphere Product (OSOP-AP)
resource.resource_description: The Office of Satellite and Products Operations, Atmosphere Products (OSPO-AP), is a collection of over 20 separate atmospheric data products that are grouped into seven categories: Aerosol, Clouds, Ozone/Atmospheric Chemistry, Precipitation, Soundings, Volcanic Ash, and Winds. Each category includes individual data products and measurements derived from NOAA's advanced satellite systems. These data are essential for monitoring and understanding atmospheric changes and how they affect the Earth’s environment. Many of the key Atmosphere products use near-real time, satellite imagery for high-resolution visual representations of cloud cover, storms, and other atmospheric features recorded for the current month. The satellites include geostationary operational satellites (GOES), polar-orbiting environmental satellites (POES), and Joint Polar Satellite Systems (JPSS). The data in the Atmosphere products are used by meteorologists, researchers, and decision-makers in support of their efforts to understand and predict weather patterns. The history of NOAA’s satellite program and instrumentation, starting in 1970, is also available at https://www.nesdis.noaa.gov/our-satellites/related-information/history-of-noaa-satellites.

publication.citation: State Climate Summaries

data_resource.time_available_comment: The Atmosphere product data available from the user interface are provided in near-real time for the current month and year. Archived data are available from the links given on the 'Data Access' page shown in the 'Data location' field below.

resource.resource_name: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Land Product (OSPO-LP)
resource.resource_description: The Office of Satellite and Products Operations, Land Products (OSPO-LP), is a collection of 20 separate data products that are subset into four categories: Fire & Smoke, Snow & Ice, Surface & Hydrology and Vegetation. The individual data products and measurements are derived from NOAA's advanced global satellite systems and provide important information about the earth's land surface that is useful for environmental monitoring, land management, agriculture, and urban planning. High-resolution visual representations of land features, vegetation cover, and land use patterns enable monitoring land surface changes, such as deforestation, urban expansion, and agricultural activities. This information is critical for supporting climate modeling and environmental impact assessments, land management decisions, and assessing natural resource sustainability.

project.project_name: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Ocean Products

resource.resource_name: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Ocean Products
resource.resource_description: NOAA's Office of Satellite and Product Operations (OSPO) provides a variety of products, including Ocean Products (OP) derived from satellite observations. The OP products support weather forecasting, climate monitoring, and marine operations. Some key ocean products include: 1. Sea Surface Temperature (SST); global and regional SST measurements for climate studies, weather prediction, and marine navigation. 2. Ocean Color & Chlorophyll; monitors ocean biological activity, including chlorophyll concentration, used for assessing phytoplankton levels and ecosystem health. 3. Sea Surface Height & Ocean Circulation; uses altimetry data to track sea level changes, ocean currents, and eddies. 4. Ocean Winds; provides wind speed and direction over the ocean, crucial for maritime navigation and weather forecasting. 5. Coral Reef Watch; tracks sea surface temperature anomalies to predict coral bleaching events. 6. Harmful Algal Blooms (HAB); detects and identifies potential HAB that can impact marine life and human health. 7. Sea Ice Monitoring; observes ice concentration, extent, and movement, supporting navigation in polar regions and climate research. These products rely on satellite sensors such as VIIRS and MODIS for data collection.

resource.resource_name: OneStop Data Search Platform (OneStop)
resource.resource_description: OneStop is an open-sourced metadata storage, management, and discovery system designed to streamline access to a broad range of environmental data collected by NOAA. The search platform has a user-friendly interface to search, discover, and access datasets that include weather, climate, oceanography, and other critical environmental science, all in one location, and uses Boolean logic and attribute filters to refine search results. The platform is designed to help improve the relevance of dataset searches, and advance both collection-level metadata management and granule-level metadata systems to handle the wide variety and scale of NOAA's data. One key objective for OneStop is to help facilitate informed decision-making and promote greater collaboration within the scientific and regulatory communities.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: Regional Hourly ABI and VIIRS Emissions (RAVE)
resource.resource_description: The Regional ABI and VIIRS Emissions (RAVE) Version 2 is an advanced biomass-burning emissions inventory developed to enhance air quality forecasting across North America. This system integrates high-temporal-resolution data from the Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) on NOAA's GOES satellites with fine-spatial-resolution data from the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) on JPSS satellites to provide hourly emissions estimates at a spatial resolution of approximately 3 km. The emissions data include various pollutants such as carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), fine particulate matter (PM2.5), sulfur dioxide (SO2, organic carbon (OC), black carbon (BC), nitrogen oxides (NOx), ammonia (NH3), methane (CH4), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). RAVE is one of six data products available in NOAA's Fire and Smoke collection found under the Office of Satellite and Product Operations Land Products.

resource.resource_description: ​The Center for Satellite Applications and Research (STAR), part of NOAA's National Environmental Satellite, Data, and Information Service, offers Global Vegetation Health Products that monitor and assess vegetation conditions worldwide. resource.resource_name: OpenLandMap
resource.resource_description: OpenLandMap (OLM) is an Open Land Data service hosted by OpenGeoHub that provides access to datasets and spatial layers that cover global land mass. OLM strives to integrate, harmonize, and process the wide range of publicly available, global legacy land data (land cover, vegetation, soil, climate, terrain data and similar) using state-of-the-art Machine Learning algorithms. The process uses a federated approach that involves researchers and programmers from a variety of organizations and institutions. Efforts are made to ensure data are Analysis-Ready and Cloud-Optimized such that they are of the highest quality, fully documented, and optimized for web-services and advanced analysis. The resulting datasets and layers are presented as a stack of gridded environmental layers that are organized around themes, such as Land Cover and Land Use, Physical Infrastructure, Geology and Soils, and Population Distribution. They are accessed from the OML portal and, unless otherwise specified, are open data, licensed under the Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL) and/or Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International license (CC BY-SA) by the OpenGeoHub Foundation. This allows users to copy, distribute, transmit and adapt data for use provided they credit OpenLandMap.org and its contributors.

resource.resource_name: Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) Spatial Climate Datasets for the Conterminous United States, Version D2
resource.resource_description: Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) contains daily gridded climate observation data from a wide range of ground-based monitoring networks. The data are displayed in the Spatial Climate Datasets for the Conterminous United States, Daily Time Series, using spatial interpolation and simulation methods. The data encompass seven primary climate elements: precipitation, minimum temperature, maximum temperature, mean dew point, minimum vapor pressure deficit, maximum vapor pressure deficit, and total shortwave solar radiation on a horizontal surface. The cover the period from 1895 to present and reveal long- and short-term climate patterns.

resource.resource_name: Safe Drinking Water Information System
resource.resource_description: The Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS) is a database maintained by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) that contains information about public drinking water systems. It tracks states' compliance with the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) and records violations pertaining to water quality and reporting requirements. The database can be queried using either basic or advanced search options based on report variables that include Public Water System (PWS) ID, PWS Name, PWS Type (e.g., Community Water System), Primary Source, Counties Served, Cities Served, Population, Served Count, Number of Facilities, Number of Violations, and Number of Site Visits. Links within the search results table are available to see more details from SDWIS reports.

resource.resource_name: USGS Products
resource.resource_description: The USGS Products are an extensive collection of publicly available resources that encompass a broad range of geological topics, including natural disaster that threaten human lives and health, natural resources, and the health/changes in earth’s ecosystems and environment, including the impact on land-use change. The information provided in the products supports decision-making on environmental, resource, and public safety issues. Some products of interest include: 1) Data products that are provided as data releases from modeling and analysis of specific datasets, or as real-time data that are collected periodically and/or from continuous monitoring in the field. Real-time data contain information about Earthquakes, Floods, Wildfires, Water conditions, Volcanoes, Landslides, and Drought; 2) Software for data modeling and analysis. Some applications include samples of processed data; 3) Web Tools for data access, analysis, and visualizations, along with access to digital data repositories, and interactive maps; and 4) Maps that present geologic, topographic, volcanic, and other related information in interactive maps. Each data product offers text search capabilities and options to filter results by data type, topic, and year.

project.project_name: Hazard Mapping System Fire and Smoke Product (HMS)

resource.resource_name: Hazard Mapping System Fire and Smoke Product (HMS)
resource.resource_description: The Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Fire and Smoke Product is a valuable tool for monitoring and predicting the behavior of fires and smoke. The continental United States is monitored 24 hours a day, year round by polar and geostationary satellites that provide near real-time data on fire location, intensity, and extent as well as smoke analysis results based on visual classification of plumes. The system displays active fire detections and smoke information for each 24 hour period, starting with the earliest observations of the day, on a fresh map of North America. This information is essential for assessing the impacts of fires on air quality, public health, and the environment.

resource.resource_name: Extreme Heat Data Pathfinder (Heat-Pathfinder)
resource.resource_description: The Heat Data Pathfinder (Heat-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth Science data collections. It provides access to reliable, global climate data records for use in assessing environmental change. Long-term data records from monitoring heat waves are used to differentiate abnormalities from normal climate conditions. The records contain robust, sustainable, and scientifically sound data of temperature, humidity, and other environmental metrics that have been collected over decades and includes a joint NOAA/NASA data set of sea surface temperatures for studying the relationship between abnormal heat events and sea surface temperatures.

resource.resource_name: gridMET
resource.resource_description: The gridMET dataset provides near real-time daily surface meteorological information from 1979 to the present. The dataset covers the contiguous United States and southern British Columbia, Canada, with spatial resolution of ~4 km (1/24th degree). Data is updated daily with the most recent available data for the previous day. The dataset provides a range of surface meteorological variables, such as temperature, humidity, and wind speed, that are used as inputs for ecological models. Additionally, gridMET provides several variables, such as the burning index and energy release component, that are used by fire management agencies to assess wildfire potential. The meteorology dataset is produced using climatically aided interpolation with a combination of a higher spatial resolution dataset (PRISM) and a long-term higher temporal resolution dataset (NLDAS-2)

resource.resource_name: TerraClimate
resource.resource_description: TerraClimate provides monthly climate and climatic water balance data during the time period from 1958 to 2024. The dataset covers global land surfaces with spatial resolution of ~4 km (1/24th degree). Variables include maximum temperature, minimum temperature, precipitation, wind speed, runoff, and soil moisture, among others. TerraClimate data can be used in various ecological and hydrological applications, such as species distribution modeling, environment impact analyses, and wildfire activity pattern studies. The climate data is produced using climatically aided interpolation with a combination of a higher spatial resolution dataset (WorldClim) and two long-term higher temporal resolution datasets (CRU Ts4.0 and JRA-55). The climatic water balance data is produced using a water balance model with extractable soil water storage capacity data.

resource.resource_name: Landscape Fire and Resource Management Planning Tools (LANDFIRE)
resource.resource_description: The Landscape Fire and Resource Management Planning Tools (LANDFIRE) program is a federally sponsored, collaborative initiative that includes a number of supporting partners, e.g., The Nature Conservancy and the U.S. Geological Survey. LANDFIRE is designed to provide comprehensive, up-to-date, consistent geospatial data and tools for use in land management and planning. It integrates data to create detailed maps and datasets that cover various aspects of the landscape, including vegetation, wildland fuel, fire behavior, and ecological conditions throughout the U.S. It is intended for use at a landscape-scale to support strategic vegetation, fire, and fuels management planning for evaluating management alternatives across boundaries. LANDFIRE is used by fire and natural resource managers, planners, policymakers, and researchers to support informed decision-making but is also available to the public.

resource.resource_name: Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS)
resource.resource_description: The Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) resource provides moderate resolution imaging data of the earth's total surface every two days. Data are collected by spectroradiometers onboard two NASA satellites that detect electromagnetic energy across 36 spectral bands ranging from 0.4 to 14.4 micrometers which allows many of the earth’s rapid biological and meteorological changes to be captured. The processed data are made available through numerous data products for studies of earth’s evolving air and land features. They are also distributed and used by multiple partners that specialize in different data products (see the MODIS web site for a list of partner distribution sites, plus the NASA EarthData portal and NASA Data Pathfinder web sites). MODIS plays a vital role in the development of validated, global, interactive earth system models able to predict global change accurately enough to assist policy makers with decisions concerning environmental protection.

publication.citation: None

project.project_name: National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN)

project_sponsor.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)

resource.resource_name: National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN)
resource.resource_description: The National Environmental Health Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) collects, aggregates, analyzes, and integrates a wealth of health and environmental data sets gathered from the national, state, and city level. The program provides comprehensive visualization tools, an API to access and contribute data, access to data at the census tract and sub-county level, and multiple indicators of environmental health. Data, presented as derived measures, are organized by indicator and content area and are searchable on the Data Explorer tool. In addition, several topic-specific “Powered by Tracking” dashboards are available. These interactive data tools provide a more complete, detailed picture of important environmental health topics all in one place.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC)
resource.resource_description: The National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC) is responsible for coordinating interagency efforts to combat wildfires and implement strategies for fire suppression and prevention in the U.S. It collects and stores a wide range of wildfire-related data (Parker et al. 2021) including information on burned areas, fire counts (Balch et al., 2020; Son. et al., 2022), and other related spatial data. These data along with coordination efforts help inform strategies for fire suppression, prevention, and mitigation. Additional resources, such as WFIGS, MODIS, InciWeb, and others, are also available from NIFC Open Data Site that offer more data and information to help manage and reduce the impact of wildfires on community welfare.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: NOAA Fire Weather (FireWeather)
resource.resource_description: Fire Weather is operated by the National Weather Service and plays a crucial role in assessing and predicting fire weather conditions. It is dedicated to providing specialized weather support to wildland fire management agencies across the U.S. to aid firefighting efforts in mitigating risks, enhancing safety, and improving operational effectiveness. Advanced weather modeling techniques and real-time data analysis are used to predict fire behavior, monitor fire progression, and inform critical decision-making processes during wildfire events. The service also relies on scientific research and data analysis to understand the impact of environmental changes on fire activity.

publication.citation: None

resource.resource_name: Open Air Quality (OpenAQ)
resource.resource_description: Open Air Quality (OpenAQ) is the largest open-source air quality data platform in the world. Real-time and historical data on a wide range of air pollutants are maintained in a comprehensive data repository that includes data for PM2.5 and PM10, Ozone, NO2, SO2, CO, and VOCs. Disparate ground-level ambient air data from reference-grade government monitors and air sensors across the globe are aggregated and harmonized on the data platform. The data are universally accessible to aid in understanding air pollution for making corrective action to improve air quality and air inequity.

publication.citation: None

data_resource.time_available_comment: Real-time and historical data from reference-grade government monitors are available from 2015 and data ingestion from air sensors were added starting in 2021.

resource.resource_name: Purple Air
resource.resource_description: PurpleAir collects measurements of PM2.5 concentrations from a public network of sensors that continuously monitor air quality. Real-time data are transmitted to PurpleAir servers where they are aggregated and made publicly available. Monitors are distributed worldwide, both indoors and outdoors, which gives a comprehensive view of regional air quality. There are almost 12,000, 24-hour averaged, PM2.5 measurements collected from collocated sensors and Federal Reference Method (FRM) or Federal Equivalent Method (FEM) PM2.5 measurements in the U.S. alone (Barkjohn et al., 2021). Studies that have evaluated performance, challenges, and data integration have indicated that, although the system has application for longitudinal ambient PM2.5 measurement, the accuracy of the data may be influenced by the location of the sensors and their installation.

resource_use_agreement.resource_use_agreement_text: All public PurpleAir data is available to download under various licenses from private, not for profit, use, to educational and commercial use.

resource.resource_name: RAP Chem Model Fields - Experimental (RAP-Chem)
resource.resource_description: Rapid Refresh (RAP) is a weather prediction system comprised primarily of a numerical forecast model and analysis/assimilation system to initialize that model, which runs every hour. It covers North America and was developed for users needing frequently updated short-range weather forecasts. The RAP Chem Model Fields (RAP-Chem) is an experimental component of Rapid Refresh Chem Plots and includes a number of forecast data, such as wind, temperature, precipitation, fire, air quality, and surface chemicals which are displayed across a static map of North America. RAP is complemented by the higher-resolution 3 km High-Resolution Rapid Refresh model, which is also updated hourly but covers a smaller geographic domain.

resource.resource_name: Smoke Sense
resource.resource_description: Smoke Sense is a program designed to engage citizen participation to raise awareness about the health effects of wildfire smoke, empower individuals to take protective actions, and improve the understanding of how wildfire smoke affects communities. Through the use of a mobile application, participants report their experiences and symptoms related to wildfire smoke exposure, providing valuable data for researchers and public health officials. The program also serves as an educational tool and resource to increase awareness and encourage people to take steps to protect their health from wildfire smoke.

resource.resource_description: The Spacially Interpolated U.S. PM2.5 concentrations (U.S.-PM2.5) from 2006-2018, version 2 was compiled by Colorado State University. It contains an updated version of spatially interpolated daily PM2.5 concentrations observed by monitors in the Environmental Protection Agency's Air Quality System and non-smoke seasonal background PM2.5 estimates. project.project_name: Time Series of Potential U.S. Wildland Fire Smoke Exposures (WFSmokeExp)

resource.resource_name: Time Series of Potential U.S. Wildland Fire Smoke Exposures (WFSmokeExp)
resource.resource_description: The Time Series of Potential US Wildland Fire Smoke Exposures (WFSmokeExp) data represent a spatial and temporal intersection of U.S. population data with wildfire smoke data from 2010 – 2019. Data from NOAA’s Hazard Mapping System’s Smoke Product are combined with data from the U.S. Census Block Group Population Centers to estimate a potential exposure using light, medium, or heavy categories. The result is a table of locations and daily smoke observations with each row displaying data for a single block group and day. The data are stored in csv format in the Harvard Dataverse Repository, a free data repository, and are useful in helping to understand the burden of wildfire smoke on communities across the U.S. The data set also encourages more research on impacts of wildfire smoke on the health of U.S. Populations.

resource.resource_name: U.S. Fire Administration (USFA)
resource.resource_description: The United States Fire Administration (USFA) provides fire incidence data through their National Fire Incidence Reporting System (NFIRS). Data are received quarterly from participating fire departments across the continental U.S. and compiled annually into Public Data Releases (PDRs), which are available for download from OpenFEMA for the years 1980-2022. The PDR is a relational database of 20 tables that are tied together by a 2-, 5- or 6-variable key. The data have been used by stakeholders to prepare for, prevent, mitigate, and respond to fire incidents and have helped reduce the numbers of fire deaths thus making communities and residents across the nation safer. Data analysis tools and additional data sets, such as Wildfires, Fire Loss in the United States, and national and state fire statistics are also available.

resource.resource_name: Wildfires Data Pathfinder (WF-Pathfinder)
resource.resource_description: The Wildfire Data Pathfinder is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. NASA collaborates with other federal entities and international space organizations to provide Wildfire information and imagery using satellite, airborne, and terrestrial systems. The resources utilized have significantly advanced the detection and monitoring of wildfires. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

resource.resource_description: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Smoke Product provides the locations of smoke plumes associated with active fires in North America in near real-time. Smoke plume locations are identified through expert visual analysis of satellite-based imagery during daylight. Additionally, smoke plumes are classified by their density as heavy, medium, or light. The HMS smoke plume data is available on a daily basis from 2003 to the present.

resource.resource_description: The American Community Survey (ACS) is a large-scale ongoing community survey conducted by the U.S. Census that provides yearly updated, detailed data on population, economics, demographics, housing, and other related information.Various guides, tutorials, and tools to support use of the data are also available at https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs/guidance.html for guidance.

project_sponsor.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)

resource.resource_name: Geospatial Research, Analysis and Services Program, CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (GRASP-CDC/ATSDR-SVI)
resource.resource_description: The Geospatial Research, Analysis and Services Program, CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (GRASP-CDC/ATSDR -SVI) database incorporates various socioeconomic factors, such as poverty, education, housing, and minority status, to identify areas that may be disproportionately affected by environmental and other disasters. By analyzing these factors, the SVI helps public health officials and emergency planners prioritize resources and interventions to support vulnerable communities and reduce disparities in disaster preparedness, response, and recovery efforts. It covers 16 U.S. census variables and helps local officials identify communities that most likely need support before, during, and after public health emergencies.

resource_reference.resource_reference_text: Flanagan, B.E., Gregory, E.W., Hallisey, E.J., Heitgerd, J.L., & Lewis, B. (2011). A Social Vulnerability Index for Disaster Management. Journal of Homeland Security and Emergency Management, 8(1). [PDF – 2 MB]

publication.citation: SVI Publications & Use Cases

intended_use.value: The CDC/ATSDR SVI is a valuable resource for assessing social vulnerability and has been widely utilized in various contexts, including disaster planning, disease outbreaks, and healthcare disparities research.

resource.resource_name: Community Resilience Estimates (CRE)
resource.resource_description: The Community Resilience Estimates (CRE) tracks the social vulnerability of every single neighborhood in the U.S. to the impact of local disasters. It measures the capacity of individuals and households to absorb, endure, and recover from the health, social, and economic impacts of a disaster such as a hurricane or pandemic. Small area resilience estimates are used to identify communities where resources and information may effectively mitigate the impact of disasters. These estimates can help facilitate disaster preparedness and aid stakeholders and public health officials model differential impacts to develop plans to reduce a disaster’s potential effects.

publication.citation: None

intended_use.value: Local planners, policy makers, public health officials, and community stakeholders can use the estimates as one tool to help assess the potential resiliency of communities and plan mitigation and recovery strategies.

resource.resource_name: Environmental Justice Index (EJI)
resource.resource_description: The Environmental Justice Index (EJI) is a high-level mapping and screen tool that provides data on the health impacts of different measures of environmental justice across the U.S. at the census tract level based on data from several federal resources. It includes an EJI ranking at the community level (census tract) based on environmental burden, social vulnerability, and health vulnerability.

publication.citation: Freelander L, McKenzie B, Lehnert E, Flanagan B, Conn M, Richardson G, Werner AK. The Environmental Justice Index: Measuring Cumulative Impacts of Environmental Burdens on Health. J Environ Health. 2023;86(2):34-37.

data_resource.time_available_comment: Data incorporated in EJI vary by data source.  EJI is released yearly and represents the past year.

intended_use.value: The EJI is intended as a high-level mapping and screen tool that can assist public health officials and others identify areas that are at risk of health impacts due to environmental factors.  EJI can serve as a starting point for further investigations into issues of environmental justice and health.

project.project_name: Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool (EJScreen)

resource.resource_name: Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool (EJScreen)
resource.resource_description: The Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool (EJScreen) is a U.S. EPA environmental justice mapping and screening tool. It provides a nationally consistent dataset and approach for combining environmental and demographic socioeconomic indicators to highlight places that may have higher environmental burdens and vulnerable populations. Users choose a geographic area and the tool then provides demographic socioeconomic and environmental information for that area. All of the indicators are publicly-available. EJScreen simply provides a way to display the information and a method for combining environmental and demographic indicators into EJ indexes.

publication.citation: None

intended_use.value: The tool may help users compare and identify geographical areas based on various social, demographic, economic, health, and environmental factors.  The resource can aid researchers, educational programs, community efforts, and general awareness. This screening tool and data may be of interest to community residents or other stakeholders as they search for environmental or demographic information. It can also support a wide range of research and policy goals. The public has used EJScreen in many different locations and in many different ways.

resource.resource_name: Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP)
resource.resource_description: The Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP) is a collection of healthcare databases, and related software tools and products, developed through a Federal-State-Industry partnership. The databases are a national information resource of encounter-level healthcare data produced from the efforts of State data organizations, hospital associations, private data organizations, and the Federal government. They enable research on a broad range of health policy issues, including cost and quality of health services, medical practice patterns, access to healthcare programs, and outcomes of treatments at the national, State, and local market levels.

publication.citation: None

data_resource.time_available_comment: Data availability each year is state dependent

intended_use.value: HCUP is intended to allow researchers, insurers, policymakers, and others to study health care delivery and patient outcomes across time and at national, state, regional, and community levels.

resource.resource_name: Kaiser Permanente Research Bank (KPRB)
resource.resource_description: Kaiser Permanente Research Bank (KPRB) is a core research resource that provides data and biospecimens to enable discoveries that contribute to health knowledge, including the prevention, diagnosis, management and treatment of disease. The KPRB data and biospecimens come from members recruited by the KPRB, as well as earlier KP biobanked collections dating back to the 1990s. The data are standardized and harmonized across regions using the KP/CESR Virtual Data Warehouse. Genomic data are also available for 103K participants who were genotyped on an Axiom Array in 2010 using four custom microarrays for each major racial and/or ethnicity group represented in the group. Additional genotyping of participants is continuing and demonstrates KP’s commitment to advancing genetic research and its potential impact on public health (Gaziano et al., 2016).

publication.citation: None

intended_use.value: The involvement of Kaiser Permanente in large-scale genomic initiatives, such as the GERA cohort, highlights its commitment to advancing genetic research and its potential impact on public health (Gaziano et al., 2016). Moreover, the engagement of KP members in surveys and studies related to genetic testing and research participation demonstrates the active involvement of the community in furthering scientific knowledge and healthcare outcomes (Burnett-Hartman et al., 2020; Carroll et al., 2019).   The resource supports research in multiple areas, such as Health Economics, Health Informatics, Health Policies, Health Services Research, Hospital Research, Implementation Science, Molecular Epidemiology, Palliative Care, Comparative Effectiveness, Pharmacoepidemiology, Qualitative and Mixed Methods,and Quality Improvement.

resource.resource_name: Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS)
resource.resource_description: Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS) is an interagency program that maps the location, extent, and associated burn severity of all large fires (including wildfires, resource benefit fires, and prescribed fires) in the U.S. Thousands of fires have been mapped and the data made available to the public [Picotte, et al, 2020]. The data include large fire perimeter and burn severity for use by the fire science community. Analysis of historical trends in burn severity across the U.S. in 2016 showed that burn area and severity have not changed substantially for most vegetation groups evaluated [Picotte, et al, 2016].
